data.title: Milestone Pharmaceuticals: Selling The News On Cardamyst Approval Makes No Sense
data.description:
Milestone Pharmaceuticals Inc. (MIST) was founded in 2003 and is based, jointly, in both Montreal and Charlotte, with its 30+ employees split between both locations. It is an early-stage biotech company that has developed a nasal spray based on a calcium channel blocker (etripamil, trademarked as Cardamyst) for on-demand/at-home use by patients to treat PSVT (paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia) and atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular rate (AFib-RVR). These are common heart conditions characterized by the sudden onset of an abnormal heart rhythm, and they affect millions of people in the U.S. and abroad. The drug works by blocking certain calcium channels in the heart, which slows down electrical signals in the atrioventricular node, thereby relaxing blood vessels. Etripamil helps the heart return to a normal rhythm by interrupting the abnormal electrical signals that patients with PSVT experience. Given some recent news, I’d like to take the opportunity to revisit this company and offer a more nuanced analysis.

Recent Developments

I last covered Milestone Pharmaceuticals in July of 2025, whereupon I recommended it as a Strong Buy under $2, on the basis of likely FDA approval for Cardamyst (etripamil). The previous submission of their New Drug Application (NDA) to the FDA was based on a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 3 RAPID trial that evaluated the drug’s safety and efficacy in patients with a history of symptomatic PSVT.

Shares at the time were $2.17 but plunged around 25% less than a week later, after three pieces of news were released:

  • A secondary stock offering, with proceeds to be used for development and commercialization efforts for Cardamyst. While the offering itself was expected to be valued at $52.5 million, the market reaction took $66 million off its market cap. This seemed overdone, especially when considering the second piece of news
  • The FDA "accepted for review Milestone’s response to issues raised in the Complete Response Letter" for the company’s Cardamyst nasal spray and provided a PDUFA date of December 13, 2025. This was for just one of the two conditions, PSVT, as a different trial was being conducted assessing the drug on AFib-RVR.
  • In the same press release, the company also announced the "extension of its $75.0 million purchase and sale agreement" with existing shareholder, RTW Investments, until the end of 2025. RTW stipulated approval before the end of the year, or else the agreement would be forfeited. This news resolved those issues by extending the deadline, thereby allowing Milestone to receive $75 million upon successful FDA approval.

The company reported during its Q2 2025 results that they generated $48.7 million in net proceeds from the stock offering, so slightly less than expected.

As such, two of these three pieces of news were “good,” one of which was “very good,” but also highly expected after previous correspondence from the FDA made no mention of efficacy or safety issues with Cardamyst but rather focused on issues that to me seemed perceptively minor. Specifically, Milestone needed to submit additional information to the FDA regarding nitrosamine impurities based on new draft guidance issued after the NDA submission and a manufacturing inspection, as their facility changed during the NDA review period.

While I was not a shareholder when I wrote the article and issued the strong buy under $2 recommendation, I put my money where my mouth is and purchased shares on the dip, getting in at $1.54. Over the following 5 or so months, they released Q2 and Q3 earnings and presented new data at the American Heart Association conference.

In total, the data from 622 enrolled patients across multiple trials showed consistent efficacy for PSVT. At the 30-minute mark, 64% of patients were back to normal, versus 31% on placebo. Also, the median time to conversion (return to normal rhythm) was 18.5 minutes across all studies—the RAPID trial showed 17.2 minutes—for patients taking etripamil versus 53.5 minutes for placebo-treated patients. The drug clearly works well, handily beating the placebo throughout the entire duration of treatment.

Safety data was considered "favorable, consistently shown, and characterized by predominantly mild, transient, and nasal-site localized adverse events"; test-dose failures were just 1.4%, suggesting high tolerability. Pooling all the studies together, the data showed that 28 of 206 etripamil users (13.6%) required an Emergency Department visit for ongoing PSVT compared with 30 of 134 placebo users (22.4%), demonstrating an 8.8% absolute risk reduction and a 39% relative risk reduction. This means that the drug would help reduce healthcare costs related to visiting an emergency department.

Milestone’s Chief Medical Officer, Dr. David Bharucha stated:

“The potential for patients to self-administer etripamil and achieve rapid termination of symptomatic PSVT episodes would represent a meaningful advance in the management of PSVT, a condition that frequently leads to emergency room visits and causes significant burden for patients.”

The consistent flow of positive news during this 5-month period helped bring the share price up over 100% from its mid-July low. MIST reached a high of $2.95 on December 11 – shareholders buying at the previous low recognized a 100% gain – but shares fell markedly the following day once FDA approval news was released.

The negative share-price reaction was a mystery to me. I am not in any position to understand what motivates stock traders and investors to sell on good news, unless they are swing-traders who are driven by short-term fluctuations rather than long-term fundamentals. I take no issue with their approach – to each their own, as they say – but as a long-term focused shareholder myself, I want to understand whether anything in my original thesis has changed. Did I miss something in my analysis?

We can see from a 1-year price chart just how volatile this stock has been.

Suffice it to say that investing in this company is not for the faint-hearted. The roller-coaster ride has continued, despite more good news last week. On January 6, Milestone announced the acceptance of a Marketing Authorization Application by the European Medicines Agency seeking the approval of etripamil nasal spray. Share rose a little over 10% over the next few days but then dropped back down below the recent $2 support level. Therefore, what investors will want to know is:

  • Are these repeated selloffs justified based on fundamentals?
  • What can we expect as shareholders over the next year and beyond?

Before I dive in, one thing that is obvious to all when looking at this chart is that each recovery following a drop has reached a new high. Perhaps, then, the stock has developed a kind of reputation for delivering quick gains for swing traders, which then drives this behavior further, through it becoming a kind of self-fulfilling prophecy. There is an argument to suggest that this volatility around news stories has helped create the dominant narrative. However, I believe that once Milestone begins generating revenue from Cardamyst, this should cease, as fundamentals are more likely to begin driving share-price fluctuations as more long-term-focused investors (like me) step in for the long haul. For now, in any event, we have yet another opportunity to reassess and “buy the dip” around the $2 mark.

Cardamyst Approval

Cardamyst is currently only approved for adults with PSVT. According to Milestone, approximately 2 million people in the U.S. are currently diagnosed with PSVT, most of whom are women (around 65%), and there is a heavy skew to the 65+ age group.

Episodes of PSVT can come on suddenly, sending heartrates to 150-250 BPM for several minutes or even hours, and they can vary in severity. PSVT can also bring upon

"disabling severe palpitations, shortness of breath, chest discomfort, dizziness or lightheadedness, and distress, forcing people with PSVT to limit their daily activities,"

according to the company. It can lead to various other problems for patients, with long-term implications. According to research, up to 27% of patients living with PSVT stopped driving for fear of temporary loss of consciousness. While PSVT is not life-threatening, it can nevertheless be debilitating.

The median experience for patients with PSVT is around 12-15 episodes per year, according to the company, despite 2/3rds of them taking daily prescription medications (such as beta blockers or calcium channel blockers) as a preventative measure. For those with underlying cardiovascular or medical conditions, such as heart failure, obstructive coronary disease, or even dehydration, the episodes might force patients to seek hospital care. Consequently, the costs to the healthcare system and the patient can be high. Once a diagnosis is made, the costs rise precipitously, as seen in the graphic below.

The current standard of care for the onset of PSVT is adenosine, which can cause chest discomfort in many patients and, in any case, this cannot be self-administered as it requires IV access. It is typically provided for patients at their first encounter with PSVT, after which the attention turns to treatments aimed at managing the condition going forward. Again, this is when it gets expensive.

At this point, the patient has several options. Some will have what is called a catheter ablation, which is a procedure involving burning or freezing heart cells to scar part of the heart and thereby "break up or insulate the electrical signals that cause irregular heartbeats." The success rates for ablations for PSVT patients in the U.S. are very high—greater than 95%, according to this study from 2007—but the take-up rate is low. It is invasive, involving wires inserted into the veins via the groin, and it cannot always be performed safely, so it is understandable that many patients may not opt for this procedure. A study >>here<< found just 14.6% of patients with PSVT had an ablation; another >>here<< found just 17.6% did; and another >>here<< found that 22% had an ablation. Reasons for not having the procedure were reported by the authors of one study:

"patient preference for conservative management due to concerns regarding procedural risk, a clinical practice gap in the diffusion and uptake of guidelines recommendations, or a disparity in care possibly due to lack of access to a cardiac electrophysiologist or ablation facilities."

The two other common treatments are performing vagal maneuvers (breathing techniques), which avoid medication but seldom work, and taking beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker pills. Some patients take these daily, but they are often ineffective and have unwanted side effects (reduced sex drive and erectile dysfunction in beta blockers, for example). Some patients do a pill-in-the-pocket approach (PIP), which is an unofficial/off-label approach where they take a pill as and when episodes occur. This can be effective in some cases, but it could be slower to react according to research from 2001 that examined the PIP approach using a combination of D/P, that is: diltiazem (a calcium channel blocker) and propranolol (a beta blocker). The study showed that "oral D/P interrupted within 2 h all out-of-hospital arrhythmic episodes in 81% of the patients," which compares similarly to etripamil, as expected. It also showed an average conversion time of 32 minutes (with a standard deviation of 22), which compares to a median time to conversion of 18.5 minutes for etripamil, across all studies. This is difficult to compare given the PIP study assessed the mean figure (and on only 32 patients), while the etripamil studies assessed the median figure across hundreds of patients.

There has not, to my knowledge, been a head-to-head comparison of Cardamyst versus the D/P PIP approach—such a study would be very helpful for investors and patients alike—but it would be no surprise if a nasal spray worked faster than a pill. In agreement were the authors of the RAPID trial (both of whom are affiliated with Milestone), and they stated of the PIP approach:

"This approach has limited efficacy that is oral therapy, potential adverse side effects (such as syncope [fainting] and hypotension), and a delayed onset and variable onset of action due to prolonged bioavailability, resulting in often slow PSVT resolution."

In practice, this means that according to the authors, the PIP approach works slower – takes longer to be absorbed into the bloodstream – and unpredictably, due to stomach contents or individual metabolism.

While investors should always proceed cautiously with claims made by the company, these are also respected scientists who have a duty of honesty and integrity to their profession. Also, clearly, the fact that patients try, and physicians recommend, such “at-home” treatments present the case for a better and more effective home remedy for PSVT sufferers who do not want or cannot have an ablation.

Enter Cardamyst, the first FDA-approved, self-administered nasal spray treatment for PSVT.

Subsequent research on the drug’s efficacy was conducted by scientists with no connection to Milestone Pharma, and the authors suggested:

"Etripamil may be suitable for immediate use in those with good responses, especially in those for whom vagal maneuvers are known to be ineffective. Alternatively, the benefit may be seen after failed vagal maneuvers or in the hospital before intravenous access, which may be obviated if PSVT is terminated by etripamil. Further, etripamil may be useful when IV adenosine has failed to terminate PSVT."

Thus, there are several instances where etripamil can be administered to benefit the patient, thereby providing a decent “window of opportunity.”

Given the healthcare costs associated with ablations or the subsequent follow-on care for those who do not have ablations – the first and second-line treatments mentioned above, alongside DCCV (direct current cardioversion) – there is evidently a market for what Cardamyst can provide. To give some figures, Milestone stated the following on their website:

"Based on market data on file, we estimate approximately 100,000 catheter ablations and 140,000 to 525,000 Emergency Department visits for PSVT occur each year and 40,000 to 120,000 inpatient hospitalizations, driving the majority of the approximately at least $5 billion spent annually in the U.S. on the management of PSVT." (My emphasis.)

WebMD suggests a cost of between $21,000 and $26,000 for an ablation, while some estimates suggest even more than this. Beta blockers and calcium channel blockers are very cheap, so it would be in Milestone’s interests to highlight how Cardamyst outperforms the PIP approach or provides healthcare cost savings overall. Given what amounts to several different options for patients, it is reasonable to expect Cardamyst to claim some of this market. The questions are: what proportion, and how quickly?

Commercial Potential

Cardamyst is set to be released this quarter (Q1 2026) according to the company, and I would expect revenue to ramp fairly rapidly. According to Lorenz Muller, Milestone’s Chief Commercial Officer – as reported by Fierce Pharma – the company plans to charge $1,649 per prescription of Cardamyst, which includes two doses. This "aligns with net revenues to Milestone of between $500 and $1,000 per prescription." This seems expensive for a nasal spray version of a cheap calcium channel blocker, but the market would tolerate the high-cost given the massive cost savings versus other treatments. Moreover, I would expect that many patients who can afford the extra cost will prefer this method of administration, a quicker-acting nasal spray versus a slower-acting pill used off-label.

The company estimated between 650,000 to 1 million patients with PSVT (of the 2 million total patients) receive treatment annually. Around half of these are commercially insured, meaning they are unlikely to reject the treatment due to a cost barrier. To me, this means between 325,000-500,000 (roughly 25% of the total number of PSVT patients) would be the base/target market for the treatment, despite the company predicting a total target market of double this (50% of the total PSVT population), as seen in the two graphics below. Based on preliminary market research with medical professionals, they anticipate a 46% cardiologist adoption rate, which suggests the nasal spray will not only be widely anticipated but also well prescribed.

It will certainly be interesting to see how this figure stacks up with real-world practices but given the probable benefits of Cardamyst versus the off-label PIP approach – if we believe Milestone’s own scientists – it seems reasonable to assume that a decent market exists. In the first instance, they aim to focus on the 25% managed by a total of around 10,000 healthcare providers, but we should take this figure with a pinch of salt, at least until a market is clearly uncovered.

Management suggests an initial market of around 500,000—targeted by 60 sales reps—but I think it will take some time to get there. Being super conservative, if we take the (lowest) $500 net revenue per prescription figure, and let’s say just 10% of the 2 million PSVT sufferers are prescribed Cardamyst by the end of 2027 (that gives them 24 months to reach this very conservative figure), this will represent nearly $100 million in annual revenue.

These set figures suggest they are likely expecting much higher revenue than I am in the first two years, but it pays to be conservative. Again, with just $100 million in revenue, and with the company’s present market cap of around $207 million, this means a 2-year forward P/S ratio of just over 2. A $1000 per script revenue figure and 10% of the total diagnosed population with PSVT gives a 2-year forward P/S ratio of 1. While the target of 200,000 prescriptions within 24 months seems very doable, these valuation figures seem more than reasonable.

Factoring the RTW Investment

When we consider what the investment from RTW represents, however, things get interesting, because the math suggests a much steeper revenue ramp for Cardamyst than I have suggested above. The company’s agreement with RTW stipulates that for their $75 million investment, they will receive in return:

  • 7% on annual net sales up to $500 million.
  • 4% on annual net sales greater than $500 million and less than or equal to $800 million.
  • 1% on annual net sales greater than $800 million

Let’s crunch some numbers to consider how much revenue would have to be generated in order for RTW to have their full investment of $75 million returned to them.

  • Royalties on first $500m (7% of full $500m = $35m)
  • Royalties on $500m–$800m (4% of next $300m = $12m).

$35m + $12m = $47m. Remainder: $28m of total $75m investment

  • To reach $28m remainder at 1% royalty, $2.8b revenue is required.
  • Therefore, total sales required for RTW to receive their $75 million investment returned in full: $800m + $2.8b = $3.6b.

It should go without saying that RTW would have invested in the business because they wanted to receive in return more than their original investment, which suggests they believe the product will generate considerably more than $3.6 billion in total sales. Let’s imagine a 100% additional return on investment (so, $150 million). Crunching the numbers again, Cardamyst would have to generate $11.1 billion in revenue over the course of the 16-year patent life to return this figure. With revenue ramping taking, say, 5 years to reach peak sales before plateauing, the ramp would look something like this:

Plotting these figures on a chart, we see the following:

For sure, these are crude projected figures, but they also imply 0% growth from year 5 onwards. That, of course, does not take into account extended indications (for AFib-RVR for example). It also suggests a steeper revenue ramp than I am expecting, but still quite conservative. In any event, we can see how this stacks up against their present valuation when we consider that their market cap is just over $200 million.

This is the bear case, but when we factor in their pipeline and expanding indications, things get even more interesting.

Cardamyst Pipeline

Milestone Pharmaceuticals can currently target adults with PSVT in the U.S., but they are hoping to broaden their market internationally and expand indications to AFib-RVR and to children with PSVT. Let’s start with the latter.

Milestone has plans to extend their approval to children, which would help broaden the market for the drug still further. Moreover, while a large proportion of adults seem content enough to tough it out through a PSVT episode at home, it is very doubtful whether most children experiencing an episode of PSVT would be accorded the same treatment by their parents, teachers, babysitters, or whoever else was looking after them at the time. As a parent myself, if one of my children developed a strange heart rhythm, at once I’d be calling 911 or rushing my child to the hospital. Thus, this quick-acting “on-demand” remedy would represent a “god-send” to many parents, relieving them of the anxiety and stress that such an episode for a child would induce.

In terms of international expansion, their progress with European approval indicates the company’s efforts to capitalize on the global market for Cardamyst. Like in the U.S., upon approval, etripamil nasal spray would become the first "rapid, reliable option" in the treatment of PSVT "outside the healthcare setting." The drug would be marketed as Tachymist in Europe, and the company are expecting a decision on approval in the next 12-18 months.

Further, as seen in the graphic above, the company has plans to extend FDA approval to treat AFib-RVR, which an estimated 10 million Americans suffer from. It is a common arrhythmia characterized by an irregular, disruptive and often rapid heartbeat. Some patients with AFib experience, along with their abnormally high heart rate, also heart palpitations, shortness of breath, dizziness, and weakness. In 2016, according to published research, approximately 5 million people in the U.S. suffered from AFib (33 million worldwide), but that first number is expected to rise to between 7 and 12 million by 2030. What does this mean?

Firstly, Milestone estimates that 30-40% of AFib sufferers experience at least one episode of RVR per year that requires treatment, which would make their total addressable market between 2.1 million (30% of 7 million) and 4.8 million (40% of 12 million).

Secondly, Milestone’s own research suggests that

"the total incremental cost of AFib in the inpatient setting, outpatient primary care setting, and outpatient pharmacy was over $8,705 per patient."

A recent report by the American Heart Association summarized the present and future cost burden of cardiovascular diseases in the U.S. and concluded that AFib resulted in $24 billion in direct medical costs and another $7 million in indirect costs in 2015 (total of $31 million), but this is expected to increase to a total of $66 billion in the U.S. by 2035.

Currently, Milestone has developed a phase 3 trial for testing self-administered etripamil for AFib-RVR. This has built off the back of their successful ReVeRA Phase 2 trial in AFib-RVR, which had its results published in Circulation: Arrhythmia and Electrophysiology.

Once the phase 3 trial is completed, the company will have to go through another NDA approval pathway with the FDA, which could take another year or so. I would expect news this year about trial enrollment and updates on progress, perhaps with early (preliminary) results. If approved (in 2027/28 in my estimation), this would represent a second indication for Cardamyst in AFib-RVR to meet their previous approval for PSVT. Success in this regard could mean potentially more than quadrupling the addressable market for Cardamyst—from 1 million for PSVT alone—to 5.8 million for PSVT and AFib-RVR by 2030. It is evident that none of this is priced into the stock at present, given the ~$2 billion market cap.

In the meantime, the company is set to see revenue ramp for PSVT, which should offset operating losses and lead to cash flow accretion.

Conclusion

Milestone has a cash balance of $82.6 million, and they will likely recognize the $75 investment injection from RTW this quarter, making a total of over $150 million in cash and equivalents. In the last 12 months, they burned through $43 million, leaving them plenty of runway ahead (3+ years at the current rate), but considering the incoming revenue from Q1 onwards, their cash burn will most likely slow. While shareholders have been hammered with secondary offerings over the past few years, I do not expect any further share dilution this year.

Milestone has a total debt of $57.2 million, of which $56.2 million is classified as long-term and due beyond 12 months. They have a net cash position of just over $25 million, but with the subsequent $75 million injection, this will jump to ~$100 million, giving them net cash per share of $0.99. This means, with shares around $2, that half of their market cap is cash! Investors picking up shares today are effectively buying the company for around $107 million. This seems incredibly cheap to me, given they could feasibly generate this same amount in revenue by the end of 2027, if not sooner, if my own predictions (and RTW’s investment due diligence) are anything to go by.

Therefore, even if there is a risk that revenue might not ramp as quickly as we project (above) due to competition from PIP calcium channel blockers—not an unfair point and certainly a risk—the reward for us being proved correct here is potentially enormous. So, given the very favorable risk-reward profile, I remain committed to my Strong Buy rating and will look to increase my position around the $2 mark.

data.title: Materials And Energy Stocks Take Early Lead In 2026
data.description:
The ledger of stock market’s winners and losers is highlighting a substantially different race so far in 2026, based on a review of equity sectors. The year is still young, and so it’s unclear if a durable change in leadership is unfolding relative to last year’s bull run. But for now, it’s obvious that sentiment has shifted in favor of shares in the materials and energy sectors, based on a set of ETFs through yesterday’s close (Jan. 14).

Currently tied for first place in 2026: materials (XLB) and energy (XLE), each fund posting a 7.5% rise. The rallies are comfortably ahead of the third- and fourth-place winners, which are also in a dead heat so far in 2026 via industrials (XLI) and consumer staples (XLP), with 5.9% gains each. The US stock market overall, via SPDR S&P 500 (SPY), is trailing with a 1.2% year-to-date increase.

The current leadership is striking following modest gains for materials (XLB) and energy (XLE) last year. In both cases, the 2025 performances trailed the sector leaders – tech (XLK) and communications (XLC) – by wide margins.

The one sector that’s extending a strong run in 2025 into the new year: industrials (XLI). Posting a competitive third-place finish last year, industrials continue to run hot so far in 2026.

It’s anyone’s guess if the renewed focus on commodities and related sectors will remain the dominant theme for the year ahead from a sector perspective, but the odds look encouraging so far, based on technical profiles. XLI, for instance, continues to reflect a solid bullish trend.

It’s premature to dismiss the AI-fueled rally in tech that consumed 2025’s headlines, but after a strong run, investors appear to be looking to relative laggards for opportunities that are less frothy.

“Hedge funds rotated billions from tech stocks into defensive sectors during Q4 2025,” the website 24/7 Wall Street recently reported. “The trend continued into January 2026.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Q4 Review: Strong Fundamentals Priced In (Downgrade)
data.description:
Morgan Stanley overview

Shares of Morgan Stanley (MS) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining about 45%. Rising market levels have supported ongoing growth in its asset and wealth management businesses, which generate attractive recurring revenue. At the same time, a volatile macro environment has led to very favorable trading conditions. These trends helped power MS to another solid result in Q4, sending shares up 2% in early trading. I last covered Morgan Stanley in November when I reiterated shares as a “buy,” given my favorable view of its fee-based revenue and strong capital position. That call has paid off with shares up over 7% since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit MS to see what investors should do now, especially with shares having hit my $185 price target before retracing modestly.

Operating leverage powers a Q4 beat

In the company’s fourth quarter, Morgan Stanley earned $2.68, which beat estimates by $0.23 as revenue grew by 10% to $17.9 billion; for the full year, MS earned $10.21, at the high end of my expectations. MS generated an excellent 21.8% return on tangible common equity in the quarter and 21.6% for the year, reflecting the attractive returns of its wealth management business, which has relatively light capital requirements. With headcount only up 1%, its efficiency ratio improved 300bps to 68%.

Net interest income increased by 15% to $2.86 billion, representing about half of pre-tax profits. Deposits were up 2% sequentially and 11% from last year to $415 billion, aided by a seasonal cash build in its wealth management business. Loans grew 17% from last year to $289 billion; this loan book is primarily to its high net-worth customers secured by their assets as well as to large corporates. Its loan book is high-quality and less exposed to credit losses than traditional banking peers. In fact, MS provisioned just $18 million for credit losses, better than the ~$60-100 million run-rate thanks to recoveries on loans it previously set aside reserves for.

M&A and debt lift investment banking

Drilling into results, its Institutional Securities business, which encompasses its trading and investment banking activities, generated $7.9 billion of revenue, up about 9% from last year. Investment banking activity jumped nearly 50% to $2.4 billion. The strength was primarily in Advisory activity. After a quiet H1 amid extreme policy uncertainty, we are now seeing M&A activity pick up, which I expect to continue in 2026. At the same time, fixed income underwriting nearly doubled to $785 million. Particularly with large tech firms needing to issue debt to finance the AI buildout, underwriting revenue should stay strong, albeit not this high, in 2026.

While equity trading rose $341 million, fixed income activity fell by nearly $170 million. As a reminder, Q4 2024 was a strong trading quarter as the Presidential Election caused significant volatility. Foreign exchange (which saw significant volatility last year) was the primary soft spot. Growing total trading revenue relative to 2024 is a still-solid outcome, even as we have seen activity normalize from exceptionally strong levels earlier this year. Thanks to revenue grow, pre-tax income rose about 12% to $2.7 billion, outpacing revenue growth, given operating leverage in the business even as MS increased its tech investment.

Rising markets and inflows lift fee businesses

In my view, its Wealth Management business is the crown jewel of MS’s franchises, and revenue surged nearly $1 billion to $8.4 billion. As advisory fees are charged as a share of AUM, rising markets mechanistically increase revenue; and given the fact accounts are charged in arrears, we should see a further jump in Q1. Beyond this, Morgan Stanley continues to win new business. Flows were an excellent $122 billion in Q4 and $356 billion for the year. As a result, AUM grew 17% to $2.75 trillion.

We are seeing this revenue result in excellent profitability with pre-tax income up nearly 20% to $2.6 billion. Margins were excellent at 31.4%, up over 240bps as advisor productivity continues to improve. Recurring advisory revenue passed $5 billion in the quarter for the first time while increased client trading supported a 17% jump in transactional revenue.

Its Investment Management unit also grew revenue 4.7% as AUM increased 14% to $1.9 trillion. AUM growth is primarily due to market appreciation. Q4 flows were seasonally light at $2 billion; still the $34 billion raised this year nearly doubles the $18 billion in 2024. Given increased competition from passive and index-linked products, I see this unit growing structurally more slowly than its wealth management business. While not the fastest growing unit, we are seeing MS improve efficiency with compensation costs actually falling $7 million to $568 million. As a result, pre-tax income jumped 10% to $468 million.

Balance sheet supports capital returns

Morgan Stanley maintains an excellent balance sheet with a common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 15.0%, which is well above the 11.8% required by regulators. As a result, MS continues to return capital to investors, buying back $1.5 billion of stock in Q4 and $4.6 billion over the full year. Accordingly, its share count fell by 1.5%. Shares also offer a 2.2% dividend yield, which I view as highly secure. I expect at least $5 billion in buybacks in 2026, and easier regulatory policy towards the supplementary leverage ratio could free up additional capital for MS to return to investors. MS’s excess capital position is about ~$5 per share, and I expect this to be worked down gradually via capital returns and organic growth.

Overall, Morgan Stanley is performing extremely well. Increased M&A activity and volatile markets are supporting strong trading results. The core of the business, managing money, is benefitting from both higher markets and a solid pace of inflows, which is also improving margins and advisor output increases. Because much of its AUM is tied to market levels, MS’s earnings will be correlated with the S&P 500, but the recurring nature of its revenue makes results relatively predictable and secure.

Fundamentals are priced in

Back in November, I saw MS earning $10.50-$11.00 in 2026, assuming flat equity markets and a normalization of trading revenue, which drove my $185 price target. Given the strong 12/31 market level (which drives Q1 wealth management advisory fees), we should see MS report further sequential growth in this unit in Q1. I expect M&A activity to stay strong this year. Trading revenue is much harder to forecast and depends on market conditions. I do not expect market volatility to be as high as in 2025, but with uncertain Fed policy and various geopolitical issues, I expect 2026 to be solid, even if it is a step down from 2025.

As a result, assuming the S&P stays within 3% of current levels, I now expect MS to earn $10.60-$11.10. Each 1% movement in the S&P outside this band is likely to impact earnings by about $0.05. I also am assuming 2 Fed rate cuts over the course of the year. At $180, shares are trading ~16.6x earnings. Given the fee-based nature of the business and strong net new assets, I view 17x as fair value, or $185-$190.

That leaves shares with ~5% of upside, combined with a secure and growing dividend. While MS is performing very well, that performance now appears to be reflected in valuation. With moderate upside and strong fundamentals, I see absolutely no urgency to sell shares. That said, I do not see the 10+% return potential I seek for a “buy” rating. As such, I am moving MS to “hold” post earnings. MS is a suitable holding in a diversified portfolio, but I would like to see shares pull back towards $170 before buying shares.

data.title: ATI Inc.: Riding The Macro-Trends To Sustain Growth And Drive Value
data.description:
Setting The Stage

At a time when we are witnessing a record commercial aircraft backlog, surging defense expenditure, and geopolitical supply chain reshoring, ATI Inc. (ATI) is positioning itself to seize the opportunity. It has transformed into what I see as a premier US-based supplier of critical high-performance materials, particularly titanium and nickel-based super alloys for jet engines and airframes. The stock is trading at $124.42 as of January 14, 2026, near its 52-week high of $125.45 and up 126.22% over the last one year, beating the S&P 500 by more than 6x.

ATI is boasting a robust Q3 2025, where it recorded $1.13 billion in revenue, marking a YoY growth of 7%, with a record Aerospace and Defense (A&D) revenue of about $793 million, which was approximately 70% of the total revenue. It had a full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance raised to $848-$858 million, pointing to a bullish trajectory. This company is capitalizing on the tight titanium supply and OEM ramps at Boeing (BA), Airbus (EADSF), and engine makers.

Despite the current rally, the market seems to underestimate the company’s earnings growth. Given this background, I am bullish on this stock and rate it a buy, driven by sustained aerospace demand, margin expansion, and surging defense expenditure.

The Broader View: Market & Industry Context

The aerospace and defense materials market is in a multi-year growth cycle, with global commercial aircraft backlogs at historic highs, as shown by Airbus with an all-time high total order book of 8,754 commercial aircraft as of the end of 2025. This trend is likely to continue with the company targeting to produce about 75 A320 planes/month by 2027 and defense procurement accelerating amid geopolitical tensions.

As this happens, the titanium demand, which is critical for lightweight, high-strength components in engines, airframes, and armor, is surging. The global titanium dioxide market is projected to grow from a market value of about $24 billion in 2024 to about $29.8 billion in 2030.

The aerospace titanium segment is growing steadily with an estimated market value of $1.8 billion in 2025, projected to grow to $2.9 billion by 2035. With this market breakdown, the US reshoring efforts bolstered by CHIPS incentives and critical mineral destination favor domestic suppliers like ATI, thus reducing reliance on foreign suppliers such as Russia/China risks.

Given the above market dynamics, the macro tailwinds include the sustained DoD budgets and EV industry demand for specialty alloys. Interestingly, the US president proposed 13% of the defense budget, $1.01 trillion, for 2026 FY, which serves as a major macro catalyst. Additionally, the automotive alloy market, valued at $25.3 billion in 2024, is estimated to grow to $45.2 billion by 2034, driven by the increasing demand for lightweight material in the industry. With EV sales estimated to account for about 30% of the total vehicle sales by 2030, it reflects on the strong adoption of EVs, which is likely to sustain the demand for lightweight materials.

ATI appears to be riding on this mega trend; for instance, in Q3 2025, its A&D revenue was a record $793 million, marking a 21% YoY growth and accounting for 70% of the total sales. This was significant growth and sustained momentum compared to an 11% YoY A&D revenue growth in Q2 2025, leading to $762 million and accounting for 67% of total sales. With the company signing a multiyear contract with major players (Airbus) in the aerospace industry, I see it as a major MOAT that would sustain its current momentum in the long term.

Diving Deeper: The Fundamental Check

Looking at this company’s fundamentals, I see a reflection of a company positioning itself in the high-margin aerospace/defense markets. For instance, the company has trailing revenue of about $4.5 billion, marking a YoY growth of 7.75%. This is a continuation of its positive revenue growth momentum since 2022. Although its growth rates have been volatile, this can be explained by the fact that the company operates in a specialty material sector tied to long lead times and other cyclical factors. For example, defense and aerospace programs involve irregular shipments tied to specific criteria like customer production schedules and program milestones. Despite these sector-specific challenges, the company has maintained a consistent positive revenue growth streak running from 2022, which is a reflection of its resilience. Important to note, comparing it to its competitors BETA Technologies, Inc. (BETA), with a trailing YoY growth rate of -1.73%, and Astronics Corporation (ATRO), with a growth rate of 6.19%, ATI outshines its peers.

Moving to profit margins, Q3 2025 highlighted a record adjusted EBITDA of about $225 million, a 20% margin. Net income came in at $110 million, marking a 33% YoY growth with a robust operating cash flow of $679.4 million in the TTM. All this speaks to the company’s strong financial performance and margins. Let me take this further and compare it to its peers. From the comparative analysis, two major observations stand out. The first one is ATI’s supremacy over its peers in margins. This, in my view, reflects on its superior cost controls and execution compared to its peers. Secondly, all three comparative metrics (operating margin, EBITDA margin, and profit margin) are on a persistent uptrend, a signal of its consistent and growing profitability, which speaks to its strong profitability and fundamentals.

Its strong fundamentals are summarized by its solid balance sheet with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.1, which is within the acceptable range of 1-1.5 and the second best among the three companies. This signals the company’s minimal debt risk, as supported by its Altman Z-score of 3.57, pointing to little or nearly no insolvency risks.

With the solid drivers and favorable industry trends highlighted above, I believe this company’s strong financial trajectory is likely to be sustained in the long term. This can be backed by the company’s projected consensus estimates with sustained double-digit earnings growth in the coming years.

Valuation

Upon reflecting on this company’s fundamentals, you realize that it has strong earnings growth, as shown by its 3-5 year EPS CAGR of 26.27% compared to the sector median of 11.66%. Considering its consistent profitability and this high earnings growth, I believe this company suits Ben Graham’s valuation method due to its emphasis on earnings and growth. I find the method to be a balanced approach since it adjusts the fair value through the use of the risk-free rate (20-year treasury yield) and corporate AAA yield as the premium risk rate. With this background, the formula is defined below.

Using this formula, I estimated a fair value of $168.48, which translates to an upside potential of 35.4%.

In my computation, I assumed a long-term growth rate of 26%, which is close to the 3-5 year EPS CAGR of 26.27%. For the RFR, I used the US 20-year treasury yield of 4.77% as of 14th January 2026, and lastly, the 20-year AAA corporate bond as of the same date was 5.31%. Given these assumptions, the results indicate that this stock is undervalued with a double-digit upside potential.

Risks

Despite my bullish stance here, investors should be wary of the risks associated with this investment. A key risk here is customer concentration. This company has a heavy reliance on the aerospace & defense customers, currently accounting for about 70% of the total revenue. This could impact its financial performance significantly should commercial aviation demand slow down or production issues arise.

Conclusion

As I conclude, I rate this stock a buy since its margins and momentum seem undervalued at the current levels despite the rally. The macro tailwinds support the estimated upside of about 35%, further backing my bullish stance.
